questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does `==` compare for Java object references?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: It tests whether the references point to the same object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It tests whether the references point to the same object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It tests whether the references point to the same object.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Content equality is normally expressed by `equals`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Content equality is normally expressed by `equals`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Content equality is normally expressed by `equals`.

answers.text: From what I've seen, two distinct String objects can be equal by `equals` but not by `==`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Two distinct String objects can be equal by `equals` but not by `==`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Two distinct String objects can be equal by `equals` but not by `==`.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does Java String immutability mean?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: A String variable can never reference a different String.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The variable can be reassigned unless the variable itself is final.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The variable can be reassigned unless the variable itself is final.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Operations such as `replace` return a new String when content changes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Operations such as `replace` return a new String when content changes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Operations such as `replace` return a new String when content changes.

answers.text: From what I've seen, a String object's character sequence cannot change after construction.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A String object's character sequence cannot change after construction.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A String object's character sequence cannot change after construction.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: what must be true when two Java objects are equal according to `equals`?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Equal objects may return different hash codes as long as collisions are rare.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The equals and hashCode contract requires equal objects to return the same hash code.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The equals and hashCode contract requires equal objects to return the same hash code.

answers.text: The way I understand it: they must return the same hash code.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They must return the same hash code.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They must return the same hash code.

answers.text: Short version: Objects with the same hash code must be equal.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Unequal objects may have hash collisions.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Unequal objects may have hash collisions.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: how are checked exceptions treated by the Java compiler?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: They must be caught or declared, subject to the language rules.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. They must be caught or declared, subject to the language rules.

answers.text: The way I understand it: checked exception handling is verified by the compiler.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Checked exception handling is verified by the compiler.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Checked exception handling is verified by the compiler.

answers.text: Short version: RuntimeException subclasses are unchecked exceptions.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. RuntimeException subclasses are unchecked exceptions.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. RuntimeException subclasses are unchecked exceptions.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: what is a consequence of Java generic type erasure?

answers.text: The way I understand it: a `List<String>` is a subclass of `List<Object>`.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Java generics are invariant; those two parameterizations are not in a subtype relationship.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Java generics are invariant; those two parameterizations are not in a subtype relationship.

answers.text: Short version: You cannot directly create `new T()` without another construction mechanism.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. You cannot directly create `new T()` without another construction mechanism.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. You cannot directly create `new T()` without another construction mechanism.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Most generic type arguments are not available as distinct runtime classes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Most generic type arguments are not available as distinct runtime classes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Most generic type arguments are not available as distinct runtime classes.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: when does a Java Stream pipeline normally execute?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: changing a stream to parallel always makes the pipeline faster.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Parallel execution has overhead and can be slower or unsuitable depending on the work and source.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Parallel execution has overhead and can be slower or unsuitable depending on the work and source.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a terminal operation triggers evaluation.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A terminal operation triggers evaluation.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A terminal operation triggers evaluation.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Every intermediate operation immediately traverses the source.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Intermediate operations are generally lazy.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Intermediate operations are generally lazy.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does `volatile` provide for a Java field?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Visibility and ordering guarantees for reads and writes of that field.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Visibility and ordering guarantees for reads and writes of that field.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Visibility and ordering guarantees for reads and writes of that field.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: It makes a compound increment such as `count++` atomic.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Read-modify-write operations still need atomic classes or synchronization.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Read-modify-write operations still need atomic classes or synchronization.

answers.text: From what I've seen, a volatile write happens-before a subsequent volatile read of that field.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A volatile write happens-before a subsequent volatile read of that field.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A volatile write happens-before a subsequent volatile read of that field.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: what does entering a Java synchronized block require?

answers.text: The way I understand it: it locks every object in the JVM.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Synchronization is associated with a particular monitor.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Synchronization is associated with a particular monitor.

answers.text: Short version: Monitor release and later acquisition establish visibility guarantees.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Monitor release and later acquisition establish visibility guarantees.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Monitor release and later acquisition establish visibility guarantees.

answers.text: I'd go with this: The thread acquires the monitor associated with the chosen object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The thread acquires the monitor associated with the chosen object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The thread acquires the monitor associated with the chosen object.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: does a Java record guarantee deep immutability?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: A record automatically generates mutating setter methods for its components.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Record components are final, and records generate accessors rather than mutating setters.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Record components are final, and records generate accessors rather than mutating setters.

answers.text: From what I've seen, yes, every object referenced by a component becomes immutable.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Record components are final references, but referenced objects may remain mutable.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Record components are final references, but referenced objects may remain mutable.

answers.text: The practical answer is: a record provides final component fields and generated accessors.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A record provides final component fields and generated accessors.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A record provides final component fields and generated accessors.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: what is a good use of Java Optional?

answers.text: I'd go with this: Representing an absent return value in an API where absence is expected.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Representing an absent return value in an API where absence is expected.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Representing an absent return value in an API where absence is expected.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: replacing every nullable field in entity classes.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Optional is primarily designed as a return type and is not universally suitable for fields.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Optional is primarily designed as a return type and is not universally suitable for fields.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, calling `orElseGet` can avoid eagerly computing a fallback.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Calling `orElseGet` can avoid eagerly computing a fallback.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Calling `orElseGet` can avoid eagerly computing a fallback.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: when is a Java object generally eligible for garbage collection?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, immediately when a local variable goes out of its source-code scope.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Eligibility depends on runtime reachability, not only lexical scope.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Eligibility depends on runtime reachability, not only lexical scope.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Cycles can be collected when the cycle is unreachable from roots.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Cycles can be collected when the cycle is unreachable from roots.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Cycles can be collected when the cycle is unreachable from roots.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: When it is no longer reachable through a chain from GC roots.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. When it is no longer reachable through a chain from GC roots.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. When it is no longer reachable through a chain from GC roots.

questions.prompt: Forum check: is `containsKey` followed by `put` an atomic compound action on ConcurrentHashMap?

answers.text: From what I've seen, ConcurrentHashMap accepts null keys and null values.

feedback.legit: Not this one. ConcurrentHashMap does not permit null keys or null values.
feedback.sus: Good catch. ConcurrentHashMap does not permit null keys or null values.

answers.text: The practical answer is: yes, all sequences of thread-safe method calls are automatically atomic.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Separate method calls can interleave; use an atomic method such as `putIfAbsent` or `compute`.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Separate method calls can interleave; use an atomic method such as `putIfAbsent` or `compute`.

answers.text: My answer: ConcurrentHashMap locks the entire map for every read.

feedback.legit: Not this one. ConcurrentHashMap reads generally do not lock the entire map.
feedback.sus: Good catch. ConcurrentHashMap reads generally do not lock the entire map.
